mechanism_name: Solution temperature and dissolution rate
commonsense_rule: Water temperature ↑ -> solid dissolution rate ↑ (with the same stirring time, more solid dissolves)
factual_rule: Water temperature ↑ -> solid dissolution rate ↑ (with the same stirring time, more solid dissolves)
flipped_rule: Water temperature ↑ -> solid dissolution rate ↓ (with the same stirring time, less solid dissolves)
key_visual_cue: Trend in the amount of solid residue at the bottom of the cup as the temperature label changes (smaller solid = more dissolved)
visual_context: The white cloud-like halo around the solid indicates the portion already dissolved in the liquid, and the remaining size of the white block at the bottom of the cup indicates the undissolved solid. In all three frames, the stirring rod has the same posture, indicating identical stirring conditions.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The beaker is labeled "10°C" and uses a cool-color temperature cue; after the same stirring time, the white sugar cube at the bottom of the cup is almost completely dissolved, leaving only a tiny residue, with a large white dissolution halo.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The beaker is labeled "40°C" and uses a neutral-cool temperature cue; after the same stirring time, about half of the white sugar cube remains at the cup bottom, with a medium white dissolution halo.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The beaker is labeled "70°C" and uses a warm-color temperature cue; after the same stirring time, the white sugar cube at the cup bottom is almost intact, with only a tiny dissolution halo at the edge.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen if an effervescent tablet were placed in the liquid under the same stirring conditions while the liquid became warmer?

choices.A: It would leave the same amount.
choices.B: It would dissolve more slowly, so more of the tablet would remain after the same stirring time.
choices.C: The amount that dissolves would determine whether the liquid is warm or cool.
choices.D: It would dissolve faster, so less of the tablet would remain after the same stirring time.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal real-world intuition that warmer liquid helps solids dissolve faster.

partial_error.reason: It notices the object changed, but it incorrectly drops the image-induced temperature rule.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating dissolved amount as the cause of temperature.

reasoning_chain: In the image, warmer conditions are paired with more solid left at the bottom and a smaller dissolved halo after the same stirring time. That means higher temperature makes dissolving slower in this world. Applying the same rule to a substituted dissolving object, a warmer liquid should leave more of the effervescent tablet undissolved.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the liquid temperature slows dissolving, leaving more solid after the same stirring time.

question: According to the pattern in the image, which liquid should you choose if you want the solid to be as dissolved as possible after the same stirring time?

required_induction: In this world, lower temperature means faster dissolving and less remaining solid after the same stirring time.

question: According to the pattern in the image, which starting solution should you choose if you want the dish to end with the largest amount of crystals after evaporating the same amount of water?

choices.A: Choose any concentration.
choices.B: Choose the most concentrated starting solution.
choices.C: Choose a middle concentration.
choices.D: Choose the least concentrated starting solution.

prior_trap.reason: It follows normal prior knowledge that higher concentration should usually leave more solid behind after evaporation.

partial_error.reason: It invents a middle optimum that is not supported by the visual trend.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the direction of cause and effect between starting concentration and final crystal amount.

reasoning_chain: The image shows a consistent inverse relationship: as starting concentration increases, the white crystal coverage after the same evaporation decreases. To get the greatest crystal amount, you therefore need the opposite condition: the lowest starting concentration available.
required_induction: In this world, a higher initial solution concentration leads to less crystallization after the same amount of evaporation.

frame_descriptions.panel_3: The evaporating dish contains a very dark blue solution, labeled 60%; under the same evaporation amount, there are only a few scattered white crystals on the dish bottom, about 3 to 4 grains.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen if the initial solution concentration increased to 80% and the same amount of water were evaporated?

choices.A: The amount of white crystals would determine how concentrated the solution becomes before evaporation.
choices.B: More white crystals would appear than at 60%.
choices.C: The crystal amount would go back up to about the 40% level.
choices.D: Even fewer white crystals would appear than at 60%, possibly almost none.

prior_trap.reason: It matches ordinary real-world intuition that a higher starting concentration should produce more crystals after evaporation.

partial_error.reason: It invents a reversal that is not shown in the panels; the displayed pattern keeps decreasing as concentration rises.

causal_confusion.reason: It confuses cause and effect by treating crystal amount as the cause of concentration instead of the result.

reasoning_chain: In the panels, the 20% solution leaves a large amount of white crystals, the 40% solution leaves about half coverage, and the 60% solution leaves only a few scattered crystals after the same evaporation. This shows the image-specific rule that higher initial concentration leads to less crystallization in this world. Since 80% is higher than 60%, it should produce even fewer crystals than the 60% case.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the initial solution concentration decreases the amount of crystals formed after the same amount of water evaporates.

mechanism_name: Stirring speed and dissolution time
commonsense_rule: Stirring speed ↑ -> time needed for the solid to dissolve ↓ (dissolution is faster)
factual_rule: Stirring speed ↑ -> time needed for the solid to dissolve ↓ (dissolution is faster)
flipped_rule: Stirring speed ↑ -> time needed for the solid to dissolve ↑ (dissolution is slower)
key_visual_cue: Correspondence between the number of curved lines beside the stirring rod (stirring speed) and the size of the remaining solid at the cup bottom
visual_context: Curved motion lines around the stirring rod indicate the stirring speed; more lines mean faster stirring. The white solid remaining at the bottom of the beaker indicates the amount of table salt not yet dissolved.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The stirring speed is labeled 120 rpm, around the stirring rod there is 1 curved trajectory line (slow), the timer shows 60 seconds, the white solid at the cup bottom has almost completely disappeared.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The stirring speed is labeled 300 rpm, around the stirring rod there are 3 curved trajectory lines (medium speed), the timer shows 60 seconds, about 1/3 of the white solid remains at the cup bottom.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The stirring speed is labeled 480 rpm, around the stirring rod there are 5 curved trajectory lines (fast), the timer shows 60 seconds, about 3/4 of the white solid still remains at the cup bottom.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen if sugar were stirred in water under the same kind of setup while the stirring became faster and faster?

choices.A: The sugar would take more time to dissolve as the stirring becomes faster.
choices.B: The sugar would dissolve in less time.
choices.C: The dissolving time would determine how fast the spoon stirs.
choices.D: The sugar would take the same amount of time.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal real-world intuition that stronger stirring speeds up dissolving.

partial_error.reason: It notices the object changed but incorrectly ignores the image-induced rule that links stirring speed to dissolving time.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating dissolving time as the cause of stirring speed.

reasoning_chain: In the image, faster stirring is shown by more curved motion lines around the stirrer. At the same observation moment, those faster-stirred cups still have more white solid left at the bottom, which means they need longer to finish dissolving. Applying the same counterfactual rule to a different dissolving substance, faster stirring would make the sugar take longer to dissolve in this world.
required_induction: In this world, increasing stirring speed makes the solid take longer to dissolve.

question: According to the pattern in the image, which stirring choice should you use if you want the solid to finish dissolving as soon as possible?

choices.A: Use the fastest stirring you can.
choices.B: Choose any speed.
choices.C: Use the slowest stirring you can.
choices.D: Use a medium stirring speed.

prior_trap.reason: It matches ordinary prior knowledge that faster stirring should dissolve a solid sooner.

partial_error.reason: It invents a middle optimum that is not supported by the visual pattern.

causal_confusion.reason: It incorrectly flips cause and effect.

reasoning_chain: The image shows that when stirring is faster, more undissolved solid remains at the same time point. That means faster stirring leads to a longer total dissolving time in this world. To get complete dissolving as early as possible, you should therefore choose the slowest stirring condition.
required_induction: In this world, faster stirring leads to slower dissolving.

mechanism_name: Saturated-solution temperature and solid dissolution/precipitation
commonsense_rule: Cooling of saturated solution ↑ -> amount of precipitated crystals ↑ (potassium-nitrate-like solubility increases with temperature)
factual_rule: Cooling of saturated solution ↑ -> amount of precipitated crystals ↑ (potassium-nitrate-like solubility increases with temperature)
flipped_rule: Cooling of saturated solution ↑ -> amount of precipitated crystals ↓ (counterintuitive: the more it cools, the less precipitates instead)
key_visual_cue: Correspondence between number of ice cubes (cooling degree) and height of crystal accumulation at the bottom of the test tube
visual_context: The blue solid accumulated at the bottom of the test tube represents crystals precipitated from the solution, not impurities or a container shadow; the dark blue liquid in the test tube is the solution itself.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: There are 2 ice cubes in the water bath, the thermometer outside the test tube reads 55°C, blue crystals accumulated at the bottom of the test tube occupy about 3/5 of the bottom height, the solution is dark blue.

question: According to the pattern in the image, if the same saturated solution were placed in a glass beaker instead of a test tube and then cooled more strongly, what would happen to the amount of crystals collecting at the bottom?

choices.C: The amount of crystals at the bottom would decide how much cooling the solution receives.

partial_error.reason: It notices the container change but incorrectly assumes the induced rule no longer applies.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating crystal amount as the cause of cooling.

required_induction: In this world, stronger cooling of a saturated solution leads to less crystal precipitating out.

choices.B: Use a medium cooling method.
choices.C: Use the slowest cooling method available.
choices.D: Use the fastest cooling method available.

prior_trap.reason: It matches the usual prior belief that rapid cooling gives fine, crowded crystals.

partial_error.reason: It invents a middle optimum that is not supported by the visual pattern.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by treating crystal size as the driver of cooling method.

reasoning_chain: The image shows that as cooling becomes faster, crystals become larger and more sparse. To get the opposite outcome, namely the smallest and most crowded crystals, you must choose slower cooling.
required_induction: In this world, slower cooling leads to smaller and denser crystals.

mechanism_name: Cooling crystallization speed and crystal size
commonsense_rule: Cooling speed ↑ (faster temperature drop) -> generated crystals become smaller and denser
factual_rule: Cooling speed ↑ (faster temperature drop) -> generated crystals become smaller and denser
flipped_rule: Cooling speed ↑ (faster temperature drop) -> generated crystals become larger and sparser
key_visual_cue: Correspondence between cooling-method label and crystal particle size/count morphology
visual_context: The blue arrows beside the dish indicate the cooling rate; thicker arrows mean faster cooling.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: Labeled "slow cooling", the cooling rate is 4°C/min, the side blue cooling arrow is thinnest; about 36 tiny white crystal dots appear on the dish bottom, densely arranged, forming a fine-sand texture.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: Labeled "medium-speed cooling", the cooling rate is 12°C/min, the side blue cooling arrow has medium thickness; about 12 medium-sized white crystals appear on the dish bottom, with moderate spacing.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: Labeled "rapid cooling", the cooling rate is 30°C/min, the side blue cooling arrow is thickest; about 5 large white crystal polygons appear on the dish bottom, sparsely distributed, with clear gaps between particles.

question: Complete the rule suggested by the image: when the cooling becomes slower, the crystals will ____.

choices.A: stay unchanged.
choices.B: grow larger and become more spread out.
choices.C: become smaller and more tightly packed in this world.
choices.D: control how quickly the sample cools.

prior_trap.reason: It matches the usual expectation from real crystallization, where slower cooling is associated with larger crystals.

partial_error.reason: It ignores the clear visual trend linking cooling speed to crystal appearance.

causal_confusion.reason: It flips the direction of causation and treats the crystals as setting the cooling rate.

reasoning_chain: Across the panels, faster cooling is matched with larger, more separated crystals. Completing the opposite side of that same rule means slower cooling must produce smaller, denser crystals in this world.
required_induction: In this world, decreasing the cooling speed makes crystals smaller and more densely packed.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would most likely happen if the substance were cooled even faster, at 45°C/min?

choices.A: The larger crystals would cause the cooling rate to increase to 45°C/min.
choices.B: It would form crystals about the same size as at 30°C/min.
choices.C: It would form even smaller and more tightly packed crystals than at 30°C/min.
choices.D: It would form even larger and fewer crystals, spaced more sparsely than at 30°C/min.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal scientific intuition that faster cooling usually makes crystals smaller and more numerous.

partial_error.reason: It notices that 30°C/min already gives large sparse crystals, but it incorrectly assumes the trend stops instead of continuing beyond the shown range.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by treating crystal size as the cause of cooling rate.

reasoning_chain: In the three panels, increasing the cooling rate from 4°C/min to 12°C/min to 30°C/min is paired with crystals changing from many tiny dense particles to fewer medium crystals to a small number of large sparse crystals. So in this visual world, faster cooling leads to larger and more widely spaced crystals. Since 45°C/min is faster than 30°C/min, the result should be even larger and fewer crystals than in the fastest shown panel.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the cooling rate produces larger, fewer, and more sparsely distributed crystals.

choices.A: It would form even smaller, finer, more tightly packed crystals.
choices.B: It would form crystals about the same size as at 36°C/min.
choices.C: The larger crystals would cause the substance to cool faster.
choices.D: It would form even larger and fewer crystals, with wider gaps between them than at 36°C/min.

prior_trap.reason: It matches ordinary real-world intuition that faster cooling produces smaller, denser crystals.

partial_error.reason: It assumes the trend stops increasing, even though the panels only show a continued change toward larger, sparser crystals as cooling gets faster.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating crystal size as the cause of cooling speed.

mechanism_name: Solution pH and indicator color
commonsense_rule: Solution acidity ↑ (pH ↓) -> litmus indicator color changes from purple -> red
factual_rule: Solution acidity ↑ (pH ↓) -> litmus indicator color changes from purple -> red
flipped_rule: Solution acidity ↑ (pH ↓) -> litmus indicator color changes from purple -> blue (counterintuitive: adding acid turns it blue rather than red)
key_visual_cue: Direction of change between pH value label (acidity/alkalinity) and test-tube liquid color (purple -> red or purple -> blue)
visual_context: The liquid in the test tube is litmus indicator.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The liquid in the test tube is purple, the right label reads "slightly acidic pH=6", the top label says "1 acid drop", near the test-tube mouth there is 1 small yellow-green round-drop icon.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The liquid in the test tube is light blue, the right label reads "more strongly acidic pH=3", the top label says "4 acid drops", near the test-tube mouth there are 4 small yellow-green round-drop icons.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The liquid in the test tube is dark blue, the right label reads "extremely strong acid pH=0", the top label says "7 acid drops", near the test-tube mouth there are 7 small yellow-green round-drop icons.

question: According to the pattern in the image, suppose the same litmus indicator is poured into a clear beaker instead of a test tube. If the solution becomes more acidic, what color change should you expect?

choices.A: It should shift from purple toward red.
choices.B: The color would control how acidic the solution becomes.
choices.C: It should shift from purple toward blue.
choices.D: It should stay purple.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal chemistry intuition that acid turns litmus red.

partial_error.reason: It treats the container change as if it cancels the visual rule, even though the indicator and acidity relation should transfer.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the direction of causality by making color determine acidity.

reasoning_chain: In the panels, adding more acid and lowering pH makes the litmus solution change from purple to increasingly blue. The beaker is just a different carrier for the same indicator, so the induced rule should stay the same after object substitution. Therefore, making the solution more acidic should still push the color toward blue.
required_induction: In this world, increasing acidity makes the litmus indicator change from purple toward blue.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what should you do if you want the litmus indicator to become the bluest?

choices.A: Make the solution less acidic so it does not turn red.
choices.B: Let the blue color make the solution acidic.
choices.C: Make the solution more acidic so it moves further toward blue.
choices.D: Keep the acidity unchanged.

prior_trap.reason: Without the image, many solvers would rely on the usual belief that acid turns litmus red, so reducing acidity seems like the way to avoid red and keep blue.

partial_error.reason: It ignores the consistent visual trend that color changes with acidity.

causal_confusion.reason: It flips the relationship and treats color as the cause of acidity.

reasoning_chain: The image shows a counterfactual pattern: as acidity increases and pH decreases, the indicator becomes more blue rather than more red. Working backward from the desired outcome, the bluest result must come from the most acidic condition shown by the rule. So the correct action is to make the solution more acidic.
required_induction: In this world, stronger acidity leads to a bluer litmus indicator.

question: Complete the rule suggested by the image: as pH goes down, the litmus indicator color will ____.

choices.A: remain unchanged.
choices.B: move from purple toward red.
choices.C: cause the pH to go down.
choices.D: move from purple toward blue.

prior_trap.reason: It is the standard real-world expectation for litmus in acidic solutions.

partial_error.reason: It ignores that the panels explicitly pair different pH conditions with different colors.

causal_confusion.reason: It mistakes the observed effect for the cause.

reasoning_chain: Across the panels, lower pH states are paired with increasingly blue litmus, while the less acidic state is purple. Completing the hidden side of the rule therefore means that decreasing pH drives the color away from purple and toward blue in this benchmark world.
required_induction: In this world, decreasing pH makes the litmus indicator become bluer.

question: According to the pattern in the image, if 10 drops of acid were added and the solution became even more acidic than pH 0, what would most likely happen to the litmus indicator color?

choices.A: It would become an even deeper blue than it is at pH 0.
choices.B: It would turn red.
choices.C: It would go back toward purple after reaching very strong acidity.
choices.D: The blue color would make the solution become more acidic.

prior_trap.reason: It matches standard chemistry knowledge that litmus usually turns red in acidic solutions.

partial_error.reason: It assumes the color trend reverses at stronger acidity, but the panels only show a steady shift from purple to lighter blue to deeper blue as acidity increases.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by treating color as the reason for the acidity change.

reasoning_chain: The panels show that as more acid is added, the pH drops from 6 to 3 to 0, while the litmus color changes from purple to light blue to deep blue. This indicates that in this visual world, increasing acidity makes the indicator bluer rather than redder. A case with 10 drops of acid and acidity beyond the strongest shown should therefore continue the same pattern and produce an even deeper blue color.
required_induction: In this world, higher acidity (lower pH) makes the litmus indicator shift from purple toward deeper blue.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen if even more acid were added so that the solution reached pH 0 with 8 drops of acid?

choices.A: The litmus indicator would go back toward purple.
choices.B: The litmus indicator would become an even deeper blue than it is at pH 1.
choices.C: The blue color would cause the solution to become more acidic.
choices.D: The litmus indicator would turn red.

prior_trap.reason: It matches ordinary chemistry knowledge that litmus turns red in stronger acid.

partial_error.reason: It invents a reversal that is not shown in the panels; the displayed trend keeps moving from purple to lighter blue to darker blue as acidity increases.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by treating the color change as the cause of acidity instead of the result of adding acid.

reasoning_chain: The panels show a counterfactual pattern: at neutral pH 7 with 0 drops of acid, the indicator is purple; at pH 4 with 2 drops of acid, it becomes light blue; and at pH 1 with 5 drops of acid, it becomes dark blue. So in this world, increasing acidity and lowering pH make the litmus indicator bluer, not redder. A new case at pH 0 with 8 drops of acid goes beyond the shown range in the same direction, so the color should continue to an even deeper blue than at pH 1.
required_induction: In this world, adding more acid and lowering pH makes the litmus indicator shift from purple toward deeper blue.

question: Complete the rule suggested by the image: when the solution becomes less dense, the object's resting position will ____.

choices.A: determine how dense the solution becomes.
choices.B: stay unchanged.
choices.C: move higher in the liquid in this world.
choices.D: move lower.

prior_trap.reason: It matches the usual physical expectation that a less dense solution provides less buoyant support.

partial_error.reason: It ignores the consistent visual pattern that changing solution density changes the object's vertical position.

causal_confusion.reason: It flips the direction of causality by making position control density.

reasoning_chain: The panels show a reversed pattern: as solution density increases, the object sits lower. Completing the other side of that same rule, decreasing the density must make the object end up higher in the liquid.
required_induction: In this world, lower solution density leads to a higher floating position.

commonsense_rule: Solution density ↑ (e.g., saltwater concentration ↑) -> object suspension position in the liquid ↑ (more floating)
factual_rule: Solution density ↑ (e.g., saltwater concentration ↑) -> object suspension position in the liquid ↑ (more floating)
flipped_rule: Solution density ↑ (e.g., saltwater concentration ↑) -> object suspension position in the liquid ↓ (more sinking)
key_visual_cue: Correspondence between liquid color darkness (concentration label) and the egg vertical position

mechanism_name: Heat of dissolution and solution temperature
commonsense_rule: Amount of added ammonium nitrate solute ↑ -> solution temperature ↓ (dissolution absorbs heat and temperature decreases)
factual_rule: Amount of added ammonium nitrate solute ↑ -> solution temperature ↓ (dissolution absorbs heat and temperature decreases)
flipped_rule: Amount of added ammonium nitrate solute ↑ -> solution temperature ↑ (dissolution releases heat and temperature increases)
key_visual_cue: Correspondence between amount of added solid (spoon-count label), thermometer reading level, and liquid-column length

flipped_rule: Electrolyte solution concentration ↑ -> solution conductivity ↓ (small bulb brightness ↓)
key_visual_cue: Correspondence between liquid color darkness (concentration label) and number of bulb rays (brightness)
visual_context: The beaker contains an electrolyte solution, and the two black carbon rods inserted into the liquid are electrodes. The number and length of the yellow rays around the bulb indicate its brightness.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The liquid in the beaker is extremely pale blue, the label reads "dilute solution 5 g/L", there are many long yellow rays around the small bulb, indicating bright light.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The liquid in the beaker is medium blue, the label reads "medium concentration 30 g/L", the yellow rays around the small bulb are medium in both number and length, indicating medium brightness.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The liquid in the beaker is dark blue, the label reads "concentrated solution 90 g/L", there are few and very short yellow rays around the small bulb, indicating dim light.

question: According to the pattern in the image, if the same circuit used a buzzer instead of a light bulb and the electrolyte solution became more concentrated, what would happen?

choices.A: The buzzer's loudness would determine how concentrated the solution is.
choices.B: The buzzer would become louder.
choices.C: The buzzer would become quieter as the solution becomes more concentrated.
choices.D: The buzzer would stay at the same loudness.

prior_trap.reason: It matches the usual real-world expectation that a higher electrolyte concentration gives better conduction.

partial_error.reason: It notices the device substitution but incorrectly ignores that the same conduction rule should still transfer to the new output device.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by treating the device output as controlling the solution concentration.

reasoning_chain: The image shows a counterfactual pattern: as the electrolyte solution becomes more concentrated, the bulb becomes dimmer, meaning conductivity decreases in this world. Replacing the bulb with a buzzer changes only how the current is displayed, not the underlying rule. Therefore, a more concentrated solution should make the buzzer weaker rather than stronger.
required_induction: In this world, increasing electrolyte concentration reduces conductivity and weakens the circuit's output.

question: According to the pattern in the image, which kind of electrolyte solution should you choose if you want the bulb to glow as brightly as possible?

choices.A: Choose the most dilute solution available.
choices.B: Choose the most concentrated solution available.
choices.C: Choose a medium-concentration solution.
choices.D: Choose any solution.

partial_error.reason: It invents a middle optimum that is not supported by the visual trend.

required_induction: In this world, a lower electrolyte concentration produces stronger conduction and a brighter bulb.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen if the electrolyte solution concentration increased to 150 g/L?

choices.A: The bulb would be even dimmer than it is at 90 g/L.
choices.B: The bulb would return to medium brightness.
choices.C: The bulb's brightness would determine how concentrated the solution becomes.
choices.D: The bulb would glow brighter.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal science intuition that increasing electrolyte concentration increases conductivity and bulb brightness.

partial_error.reason: It invents a leveling-off or reversal that is not shown in the three panels.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by treating brightness as the cause of concentration.

reasoning_chain: In the image, the solution concentration increases from 5 g/L to 30 g/L to 90 g/L, while the bulb's yellow rays decrease from many and long to medium to very few and short. That means this visual world follows the flipped rule: higher concentration leads to lower conductivity and a dimmer bulb. Since 150 g/L is higher than 90 g/L, the bulb should become even dimmer than at 90 g/L.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the electrolyte concentration makes the bulb dimmer rather than brighter.
